findings.description: _is_expired is inverted: 'return age < self.ttl' reports fresh entries as expired (evicting them on first L1 read so L1 never serves a hit) and reports past-TTL entries as fresh (serving stale data forever, never evicting/refreshing); should be 'age >= self.ttl'.

None is overloaded as both the cache-miss sentinel and a valid stored value, so get_or_compute ('if cached is not None') recomputes on every call for keys whose computed value is genuinely None, never serving the cached None (cache penetration for None-valued keys).

Bare 'del self._local[key]' (and unsynchronized mutation of _local/stats generally) can raise KeyError under concurrent access typical of a web service when two threads evict the same expired key; should use pop(key, None) and/or locking.

stats.local_entries is updated only in set(), not in get()'s insert (line 61) or delete (line 54) paths, so the CacheStats.local_entries field drifts stale for any direct reader (masked only because export_stats recomputes len(self._local) directly).

set() writes L1 before L2; if redis.setex raises, L1 already holds the new value that L2 lacks (partial-failure inconsistency with no rollback), and a two-tier cache normally writes the source-of-truth L2 first.
invalidate() pops L1 then deletes L2; if redis.delete raises, L1 is cleared but L2 retains the value, allowing the next get() to re-populate L1 from the stale L2 entry.

warm() returns len(entries) as 'number of entries written'; accurate on the success path (dict keys are unique) but misreports if a mid-loop set() fails — though in that case the exception propagates before return, so impact is minimal.
pickle.loads on bytes read from Redis is a deserialization/RCE vector if Redis is attacker-writable or shared; noted in passing — detailed assessment deferred to critic-security.
